schools.summary: Hannah's song of exultation, rejoicing in the LORD and his salvation: 'and Hannah prayed, and said, My heart rejoiceth in the LORD, mine horn is exalted in the LORD: my mouth is enlarged over mine enemies; because I rejoice in thy salvation' — Hannah's prayer became a song of triumph in God (the and Hannah prayed, and said, My heart rejoiceth in the LORD, mine horn is exalted in the LORD, the rejoicing, the my mouth is enlarged over mine enemies; because I rejoice in thy salvation, the salvation, the figure, the soul exulting not in its gift but in the Giver and his salvation, the truth that Hannah rejoiced in the LORD himself and his salvation, Psalm 13:5, the figure of the believer who triumphs in God and his deliverance, the song of Hannah, the joy in salvation, vv.1:27); Hannah's heart rejoicing in the LORD (the joy, Psalm 13:5) — the exultation (rejoiceth in the LORD); 'I rejoice in thy salvation' (Hannah's joy in God's salvation, the deliverance, the figure, the believer who triumphs in God and his deliverance, the providence, the song, the holiness of God to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah confessing the unique holiness and majesty of God: 'there is none holy as the LORD: for there is none beside thee: neither is there any rock like our God' — Hannah confessed God's unrivalled holiness and strength (the There is none holy as the LORD, the holiness, the for there is none beside thee: neither is there any rock like our God, none beside him, the figure, the incomparable holiness and steadfast strength of God, the unique majesty, the truth that none is holy or strong as the LORD, the only God and rock, Deuteronomy 32:4, the figure of the God who alone is holy and the rock of his people, the holiness of God, the rock unrivalled, vv.1); the unrivalled holiness of God (the majesty, Deuteronomy 32:4) — the holiness (none holy as the LORD); 'neither is there any rock like our God' (God the unrivalled rock of his people, the strength, the figure, the God who alone is holy and the rock of his people, the providence, the holiness, the rebuke of the proud to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah's rebuke of proud speech, for God weighs all actions: 'talk no more so exceeding proudly; let not arrogancy come out of your mouth: for the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed' — Hannah warned the proud, since God knows and weighs all (the Talk no more so exceeding proudly; let not arrogancy come out of your mouth, the rebuke of pride, the for the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed, God weighs actions, the figure, the warning against pride before the all-knowing God who weighs every deed, the truth that God knows and weighs all actions, so pride is folly, Proverbs 16:2, the figure of the omniscient God before whom pride is silenced, the rebuke of pride, actions weighed, vv.2); Hannah rebuking proud speech (the warning, Proverbs 16:2) — the rebuke (talk no more proudly); 'the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed' (God knowing and weighing all deeds, the omniscience, the figure, the omniscient God before whom pride is silenced, the providence, the rebuke, the reversals God works to follow, vv.4-5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God breaking the mighty and strengthening the weak: 'the bows of the mighty men are broken, and they that stumbled are girded with strength' — God reverses the lot of the strong and the weak (the The bows of the mighty men are broken, the mighty broken, the and they that stumbled are girded with strength, the weak strengthened, the figure, the divine reversal that breaks the strong and girds the weak, the truth that God breaks the mighty and strengthens the feeble, Psalm 37:17, the figure of the God who reverses the conditions of men at his pleasure, the reversal of God, the mighty and the weak, vv.3); God breaking the mighty (the reversal, Psalm 37:17) — the breaking (bows broken); 'they that stumbled are girded with strength' (the weak girded by God, the reversal, the figure, the God who reverses the conditions of men at his pleasure, the providence, the reversal, the full and the hungry to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's reversals: the full made needy, the hungry filled, the barren made fruitful: 'they that were full have hired out themselves for bread; and they that were hungry ceased: so that the barren hath born seven; and she that hath many children is waxed feeble' — God reverses fullness and hunger, fruitfulness and barrenness (the They that were full have hired out themselves for bread; and they that were hungry ceased, the full and the hungry, the so that the barren hath born seven; and she that hath many children is waxed feeble, the barren and the fruitful, the figure, the divine reversal in fortune and family, the barren made fruitful as Hannah, the truth that God reverses fullness, hunger, and barrenness, Psalm 113:9, the figure of the God who lifts the low and humbles the high in every estate, the reversals of God, the barren made fruitful, vv.4); the full made needy and the hungry filled (the reversal, Psalm 113:9) — the reversal (the full and the hungry); 'the barren hath born seven; and she that hath many children is waxed feeble' (the barren made fruitful, the fruitful made feeble, Hannah's own case, the figure, the God who lifts the low and humbles the high, the providence, the reversals, God's power over life and death to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah's confession that the LORD kills and makes alive, brings down to the grave and raises up: 'the LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up' — the sovereignty over life and death — 'the LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up': Hannah ascribes to God the absolute disposal of life and death, the depths and the heights (the witnesses noting the significance — the great confession of God's sovereign power over the whole estate of man, that he is Lord of life and death, who kills and makes alive, brings down even to the grave and brings up again, so that no condition is beyond his reach, neither the lowest depth of death nor the most hopeless ruin, but he can bring down the living to death and raise up the dead to life, the entire compass of man's being subject to his will, the Lord of life and death, the depths and the heights his, no estate beyond his power to change, the lesson — that God holds absolute sway over life and death, and the believer may trust him in the lowest extremity, 'see now that I, even I, am he... I kill, and I make alive,' Deuteronomy 32:39, the truth that God can raise even from death and the grave, so that no affliction is past his help, 'thou which hast shewed me great and sore troubles shalt quicken me again, and shalt bring me up again from the depths of the earth,' Psalm 71:20, the figure — the God who is Lord of death and resurrection, fulfilled in him who has the keys of death and the grave and raises the dead, 'I am he that liveth, and was dead... and have the keys of hell and of death,' Revelation 1:18, the truth that the power that kills and makes alive is the ground of hope in every extremity, even of resurrection, the figure of the absolute sovereignty of God over life and death, the lowest depths and the highest deliverances, the call to trust the God who is Lord of life and death, the assurance that no depth is beyond the reach of him who brings up from the grave); God killing and making alive, the absolute disposal of life in his hand, no estate beyond his power (the Lord of life and death, Deuteronomy 32:39) — the sovereignty (killeth and maketh alive); 'he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up' (God bringing down even to the grave and bringing up again, no depth of death or ruin beyond his reach, the ground of hope even of resurrection, fulfilled in him who has the keys of death, the God of death and resurrection, Psalm 71:20; Revelation 1:18) — the figure (the God who is Lord of death and resurrection, the providence, the sovereignty, the poor raised from the dust to follow, vv.7-8) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the LORD killeth, and maketh alive' confess?

sides.position: The absolute sovereignty of God over life and death, the lowest depths and the highest deliverances - Lord of life and death who kills and makes alive, brings down to the grave and brings up, no condition beyond his reach, the believer trusting him in the lowest extremity (Deuteronomy 32:39), God able to raise from death so no affliction is past his help (Psalm 71:20), the God who has the keys of death (Revelation 1:18)

themes: The LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up.

the believer trusting him in the lowest extremity, God able to raise from death and the grave so no affliction is past his help, the God who has the keys of death (Deuteronomy 32:39; Psalm 71:20; Revelation 1:18)

schools.summary: God's making poor and rich, bringing low and lifting up: 'the LORD maketh poor, and maketh rich: he bringeth low, and lifteth up' — God disposes of riches and poverty, abasement and exaltation (the The LORD maketh poor, and maketh rich, poor and rich, the he bringeth low, and lifteth up, low and lifted up, the figure, the divine hand that disposes every estate, poverty and wealth, low and high, the truth that God makes poor and rich, abases and exalts, Psalm 75:7, the figure of the God who orders all the conditions of men, the disposal of God, the low and the lifted, vv.6); God making poor and rich (the disposal, Psalm 75:7) — the disposal (poor and rich); 'he bringeth low, and lifteth up' (God abasing and exalting, the disposal, the figure, the God who orders all the conditions of men, the providence, the disposal, the poor raised to princes to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That God raises the poor from the dust to set them among princes: 'he raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth up the beggar from the dunghill, to set them among princes, and to make them inherit the throne of glory: for the pillars of the earth are the LORD's, and he hath set the world upon them' — the lifting of the lowly to glory — 'he raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth up the beggar from the dunghill, to set them among princes, and to make them inherit the throne of glory: for the pillars of the earth are the LORD's': God lifts the lowest from the dust to the highest honour, for all the earth is his (the witnesses noting the significance — the wonderful condescension and sovereign grace of God, who stoops to the very dust and the dunghill, to the poorest and most abject, and raises them to sit among princes and inherit a throne of glory, the greatest exaltation from the lowest estate, and this not by chance but by his own purpose and power, founded on his sovereign right as the Maker and upholder of all, for the pillars of the earth are his and he set the world upon them, the lowest raised to the highest, the dunghill to the throne, the sovereign grace that founds all on his power as Creator, the lesson — that God delights to exalt the lowly and abase the proud, choosing the poor and despised to inherit glory, 'hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom?' James 2:5, the truth that the God who upholds the world can raise the meanest to glory, his power the ground of all his grace, 'he hath put down the mighty from their seats, and exalted them of low degree,' Luke 1:52, the figure — the sovereign grace that lifts the lowliest of men to the throne of glory, fulfilled in the gospel that raises beggars to be sons and heirs, 'the Spirit itself beareth witness... that we are the children of God... and joint-heirs with Christ,' Romans 8:16-17, the truth that the exaltation of the lowly rests on the almighty power of the Creator, the figure of the God who lifts the lowliest to glory, founding all on his sovereign power, the call to trust the God who exalts the humble, the assurance that the poorest he raises to a throne of glory); God raising the poorest from the dust and dunghill to sit among princes and inherit glory, the greatest exaltation from the lowest estate by his sovereign grace (the lowly lifted to glory, James 2:5) — the exaltation (raiseth the poor to princes); 'for the pillars of the earth are the LORD's, and he hath set the world upon them' (all this rests on God's sovereign right as Maker and upholder of the world, the pillars of the earth his, his power the ground of his grace, the Creator's power the ground of grace, Luke 1:52; Romans 8:16-17) — the figure (the sovereign grace that lifts the lowliest to the throne of glory, the providence, the exaltation, the keeping of the saints' feet to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does God raising the poor from the dust to princes signify?

sides.position: The God who lifts the lowliest to glory, founding all on his sovereign power as Maker and upholder of the world - God stooping to the dust and dunghill and raising the abject to inherit a throne of glory, founded on his right as Maker, choosing the poor to inherit the kingdom (James 2:5), the God who upholds the world raising the meanest to glory (Luke 1:52), the grace that lifts beggars to be heirs with Christ (Romans 8:16-17)

themes: He raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth up the beggar from the dunghill, to set them among princes, and to make them inherit the throne of glory: for the pillars of the earth are the LORD's, and he hath set the world upon them.

the wonderful condescension and sovereign grace of God, who stoops to the dust and the dunghill and raises the abject to sit among princes and inherit a throne of glory, founded on his sovereign right as Maker and upholder of all

schools.summary: God keeping the feet of his saints while the wicked are silenced: 'he will keep the feet of his saints, and the wicked shall be silent in darkness; for by strength shall no man prevail' — God preserves the godly and silences the wicked, for no human strength prevails (the He will keep the feet of his saints, the saints kept, the and the wicked shall be silent in darkness; for by strength shall no man prevail, the wicked silenced, the figure, the God who guards his own and brings the wicked to nothing, the helplessness of mere strength, the truth that God keeps the saints and silences the wicked, no strength availing, Psalm 121:3, the figure of the God who preserves his people and confounds the proud, the keeping of the saints, the wicked silenced, vv.8); God keeping the feet of his saints (the preservation, Psalm 121:3) — the keeping (the saints' feet); 'the wicked shall be silent in darkness; for by strength shall no man prevail' (the wicked brought to nothing, no strength availing, the helplessness, the figure, the God who preserves his people and confounds the proud, the providence, the keeping, the breaking of God's adversaries to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah's prophetic close, that the LORD will judge the earth and exalt the horn of his anointed: 'the adversaries of the LORD shall be broken to pieces; out of heaven shall he thunder upon them: the LORD shall judge the ends of the earth; and he shall give strength unto his king, and exalt the horn of his anointed' — the Messianic prophecy — 'the adversaries of the LORD shall be broken to pieces... the LORD shall judge the ends of the earth; and he shall give strength unto his king, and exalt the horn of his anointed': Hannah closes her song with the judgment of the earth and the exaltation of God's King and Anointed (the witnesses noting the significance — the remarkable prophetic reach of Hannah's song, that, lifting from her own deliverance to the whole purpose of God, she foresees the breaking of God's adversaries, his universal judgment of the earth, and the giving of strength to his King and the exaltation of the horn of his 'Anointed' (Messiah), the first time in Scripture that word is used of the coming King, so that Hannah, before there was a king in Israel, prophesies the kingdom of God's Anointed, which finds its true fulfilment not in Saul or David only but in the Messiah, the Christ, the King of God's appointing whose horn is exalted forever, the song rising to the Messianic kingdom, the first naming of the Anointed, the judgment of the earth and the triumph of God's King, the lesson — that the whole purpose of God moves toward the kingdom of his Anointed, who shall judge the earth and reign, 'yet have I set my king upon my holy hill of Zion... thou art my Son,' Psalm 2:6-7, the truth that God will exalt his Anointed and give him the kingdom and the judgment of the world, 'the LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand,' Psalm 110:1, the figure — the exaltation of the Messiah-King, fulfilled in Christ whom God has highly exalted and to whom he has given all judgment, 'God also hath highly exalted him, and given him a name which is above every name,' Philippians 2:9, the truth that Hannah's song, like Mary's, magnifies the God who exalts the lowly and crowns his Anointed, the figure of the King and the Anointed One, the Messiah-King whose kingdom Hannah foresees, the call to look to the exalted Anointed of God, the assurance that God will exalt the horn of his Christ forever); God breaking his adversaries and judging the ends of the earth, the universal sovereignty and judgment of God (the judgment of the earth, Psalm 2:6-7) — the judgment (judge the ends of the earth); 'and he shall give strength unto his king, and exalt the horn of his anointed' (God giving strength to his King and exalting the horn of his Anointed, Messiah, the first naming of the coming King, fulfilled not in Saul or David only but in the Messiah, the Christ highly exalted, the Messiah-King, Psalm 110:1; Philippians 2:9) — the figure (the exaltation of the Messiah-King, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the prophecy, Samuel ministering and the kingdom to come to follow, vv.11) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Hannah's 'exalt the horn of his anointed' foresee?

sides.position: The King and the Anointed One, the first mention of the Messiah-King whose kingdom Hannah foresees - Hannah before there was a king in Israel prophesying the kingdom of God's Anointed (Messiah), the first naming of the coming King, fulfilled not in Saul or David only but in the Messiah whose horn is exalted forever, the purpose of God moving toward the kingdom of his Anointed (Psalm 2:6-7), God exalting his Anointed (Psalm 110:1), Christ highly exalted (Philippians 2:9)

themes: The adversaries of the LORD shall be broken to pieces; out of heaven shall he thunder upon them: the LORD shall judge the ends of the earth; and he shall give strength unto his king, and exalt the horn of his anointed.

the prophetic reach of Hannah's song, foreseeing the breaking of God's adversaries, his universal judgment, and the exaltation of the horn of his Anointed (Messiah), the first time that word is used of the coming King

Hannah prophesying the kingdom of God's Anointed before there was a king in Israel, fulfilled in the Messiah whose horn is exalted forever, the purpose of God moving toward the kingdom of his Anointed (Psalm 2:6-7; Psalm 110:1; Philippians 2:9)

schools.summary: Elkanah returning home and the child Samuel ministering before the LORD: 'and Elkanah went to Ramah to his house. schools.summary: Eli's sons were wicked men who knew not the LORD: 'now the sons of Eli were sons of Belial; they knew not the LORD' — Eli's sons, though priests, were wicked and godless (the Now the sons of Eli were sons of Belial, the sons of Belial, the they knew not the LORD, knew not the LORD, the figure, the priests who served the altar yet knew not God, the corruption in the sanctuary, the truth that Eli's sons were wicked, knowing not the LORD whose priests they were, Jeremiah 2:8, the figure of the ministers who serve in office yet are strangers to God, the wicked sons, the LORD unknown, vv.11); Eli's sons as sons of Belial (the corruption, Jeremiah 2:8) — the wickedness (sons of Belial); 'they knew not the LORD' (the priests strangers to the God they served, the godlessness, the figure, the ministers who serve in office yet are strangers to God, the providence, the wickedness, their greed at the sacrifices to follow, vv.13-17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The corrupt custom of Eli's sons in seizing the sacrifices: 'and the priests' custom with the people was, that, when any man offered sacrifice, the priest's servant came, while the flesh was in seething, with a fleshhook of three teeth in his hand' — Eli's sons had a greedy custom of taking the people's sacrifices (the and the priests' custom with the people was, that, when any man offered sacrifice, the custom, the the priest's servant came, while the flesh was in seething, with a fleshhook of three teeth in his hand, the fleshhook, the figure, the greedy abuse of the priestly office, the sacrifice seized, the truth that Eli's sons took the offerings by force and custom, against the law, Leviticus 7:34, the figure of the corruption that turns God's service to private gain, the priests' custom, the sacrifice seized, vv.12); the corrupt custom of the priests (the abuse, Leviticus 7:34) — the custom (when any man offered); 'the priest's servant came... with a fleshhook of three teeth in his hand' (the servant seizing the flesh, the greed, the figure, the corruption that turns God's service to private gain, the providence, the custom, the seizing of the raw flesh to follow, vv.14-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The priest's servant seizing whatever the fleshhook brought up: 'and he struck it into the pan, or kettle, or caldron, or pot; all that the fleshhook brought up the priest took for himself. So they did in Shiloh unto all the Israelites that came thither' — the priest's servant took for himself whatever the hook brought up, robbing all the worshippers (the and he struck it into the pan, or kettle, or caldron, or pot; all that the fleshhook brought up the priest took for himself, the seizing, the So they did in Shiloh unto all the Israelites that came thither, to all who came, the figure, the greedy seizing of the people's offerings, the robbery of the worshippers, the truth that the priests robbed all who came to sacrifice, Micah 3:11, the figure of the corruption that preys on the worshippers of God, the seizing, the robbery of all, vv.13); the priest taking all the hook brought up (the robbery, Micah 3:11) — the seizing (took for himself); 'So they did in Shiloh unto all the Israelites that came thither' (the robbery practised on all the worshippers, the extent, the figure, the corruption that preys on the worshippers of God, the providence, the seizing, the demand for raw flesh to follow, vv.15-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The priests demanding raw flesh before the fat was burnt to God: 'also before they burnt the fat, the priest's servant came, and said to the man that sacrificed, Give flesh to roast for the priest; for he will not have sodden flesh of thee, but raw' — the priests demanded their portion before God's portion was offered (the Also before they burnt the fat, the priest's servant came, and said to the man that sacrificed, Give flesh to roast for the priest, the demand, the for he will not have sodden flesh of thee, but raw, raw flesh demanded, the figure, the greed that seized its portion before God's, the contempt of the offering, the truth that the priests took their part before the fat was burnt to God, against the law, Leviticus 3:16, the figure of the irreverence that puts self before God in his own service, the demand of the priests, the fat dishonoured, vv.13); the priests demanding flesh before the fat was burnt (the greed, Leviticus 3:16) — the demand (give flesh to roast); 'he will not have sodden flesh of thee, but raw' (the priest demanding raw flesh, his own way, the irreverence, the figure, the irreverence that puts self before God in his own service, the providence, the demand, the threat of force to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The priests taking the sacrifice by force if refused: 'and if any man said unto him, Let them not fail to burn the fat presently, and then take as much as thy soul desireth; then he would answer him, Nay; but thou shalt give it me now: and if not, I will take it by force' — the priests would take the offering by force if the worshipper objected (the and if any man said unto him, Let them not fail to burn the fat presently, and then take as much as thy soul desireth, the worshipper's plea, the then he would answer him, Nay; but thou shalt give it me now: and if not, I will take it by force, the violence, the figure, the violent greed that would rob God and man, the threat of force, the truth that the priests would take the offering by force, despising God's portion, Micah 2:2, the figure of the violence that seizes by force what God forbids, the threat of the priests, the force used, vv.15); the worshipper's plea to burn the fat first (the reverence, Micah 2:2) — the plea (burn the fat first); 'I will take it by force' (the priest threatening violence, the robbery, the figure, the violence that seizes by force what God forbids, the providence, the violence, the great sin before the LORD to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if any man said unto him, Let them not fail to burn the fat presently, and then take as much as thy soul desireth; then he would answer him, Nay; but thou shalt give it me now: and if not, I will take it by force.

schools.summary: Eli's warning that there is no intercessor for sin against the LORD, and that his sons heard not because the LORD would slay them: 'if one man sin against another, the judge shall judge him: but if a man sin against the LORD, who shall intreat for him? Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto the voice of their father, because the LORD would slay them' — the no-intercessor warning and the judicial hardening — 'if one man sin against another, the judge shall judge him: but if a man sin against the LORD, who shall intreat for him? Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto the voice of their father, because the LORD would slay them': Eli warns that sin against God leaves no human intercessor, and his sons' deafness is the sign of God's purpose to destroy them (the witnesses noting the significance — two solemn truths joined: first, that while a man's sin against his neighbour may be judged and atoned, a presumptuous sin against the LORD himself leaves no creature to intercede, none to plead the cause of the obstinate despiser, and second, the dread word that the sons hearkened not 'because the LORD would slay them,' that is, their hardness and deafness were the very mark and means of the judgment God had determined, the obstinate sinner judicially given up to the destruction he had earned, his refusal to hear the sentence of God upon him, the sin against God beyond human intercession, the deafness the sign of judgment, the LORD's purpose to slay working through their own hardness, the lesson — that there is a sin and a hardness which is the token of God's abandoning judgment, the sinner given over to his own deafness, 'he, that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed, and that without remedy,' Proverbs 29:1, the truth that God may judicially harden the obstinate, their own refusal the instrument of their ruin, 'but they refused to hearken... that they might not hear... therefore came a great wrath,' Zechariah 7:11-12, the figure — the awful giving-up of the despiser to his sin and its doom, fulfilled in those whom God gives over to a reprobate mind, 'even as they did not like to retain God... God gave them over,' Romans 1:28, the truth that to despise the warnings of God is to be sealed in the judgment one will not flee, the figure of the judicial hardening by which God gives up the obstinate sinner to the destruction he has earned, the call to hear God's warnings while there is remedy, the warning that the deaf ear may be the seal of doom); Eli warning that sin against the LORD himself leaves no creature to intercede, none to plead for the obstinate despiser (the sin beyond intercession, Proverbs 29:1) — the warning (who shall intreat for him); 'Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto the voice of their father, because the LORD would slay them' (the sons' deafness being the mark and means of God's purpose to destroy them, the obstinate given up judicially to their own hardness, refusing to hear the sentence upon them, God giving over the despiser, the hardening unto judgment, Zechariah 7:11-12; Romans 1:28) — the figure (the awful giving-up of the despiser to his sin and its doom, the providence, the hardening, the man of God's prophecy against Eli's house to follow, vv.27-36) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: God's great principle, that them that honour him he will honour, and they that despise him shall be lightly esteemed: 'wherefore the LORD God of Israel saith, I said indeed that thy house, and the house of thy father, should walk before me for ever: but now the LORD saith, Be it far from me; for them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed' — the law of divine requital — 'I said indeed that thy house... should walk before me for ever: but now the LORD saith, Be it far from me; for them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed': God revokes the standing promise to Eli's house and lays down the abiding principle of his dealings (the witnesses noting the significance — the great and unchanging rule of God's moral government, declared in the very act of judging Eli's house, that God will honour those who honour him and lightly esteem, despise, bring to contempt, those who despise him, so that the priestly promise, though it had seemed perpetual, is forfeited by contempt of God, for no privilege or office can stand where God is despised, and the law of requital is sure: honour for honour, contempt for contempt, the conditional nature of every privilege under this rule, God's honour the measure of his blessing, the despiser brought to shame, the lesson — that God deals with men according as they deal with him, exalting those who honour him and abasing those who despise him, 'the LORD taketh pleasure in them that fear him... but a froward heart shall depart from me,' Psalm 147:11, the truth that to honour God is the way to be honoured of him, and to despise him is to be brought to contempt, 'them that honour me,' fulfilled in him who said 'if any man serve me, him will my Father honour,' John 12:26, the figure — the sure law of God's requital, that the path of honouring God leads to honour and the way of despising him to shame, fulfilled in the final judgment when God shall say 'Well done' to the faithful and reject the despiser, 'whosoever shall confess me before men, him will I confess... but whosoever shall deny me... him will I also deny,' Matthew 10:32-33, the truth that the highest privilege cannot stand without the honouring of God, the figure of the unchanging law of God's government, that he requites honour with honour and contempt with contempt, the call to honour God that we may be honoured of him, the warning that to despise God is to be brought low); Matthew 10:32-33) — the figure (the sure law of God's requital that leads to honour or shame, the providence, the principle, the sentence on Eli's house to follow, vv.31-36) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

the one spared a grief and the house dying in youth

schools.summary: The sign, that Hophni and Phinehas would die in one day: 'and this shall be a sign unto thee, that shall come upon thy two sons, on Hophni and Phinehas; in one day they shall die both of them' — God gave the death of both sons in one day as the sign of the whole judgment (the and this shall be a sign unto thee, that shall come upon thy two sons, on Hophni and Phinehas, the sign, the in one day they shall die both of them, both dying in one day, the figure, the pledge and earnest of the judgment, the two sons dying together, the truth that the death of both sons in one day would confirm the prophecy, 1 Samuel 4:11, the figure of the sign that seals the certainty of God's word, the sign to Eli, the sons dying together, vv.31); the death of the sons as the sign (the pledge, 1 Samuel 4:11) — the sign (upon thy two sons); 'in one day they shall die both of them' (both dying in one day, the confirmation, the figure, the sign that seals the certainty of God's word, the providence, the sign, the raising of a faithful priest to follow, vv.35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And this shall be a sign unto thee, that shall come upon thy two sons, on Hophni and Phinehas; in one day they shall die both of them.
The sign, that Hophni and Phinehas would die in one day
God giving the death of both sons in one day as the sign of the whole judgment
the sign that seals the certainty of God's word (1 Samuel 4:11)

schools.summary: God's promise to raise up a faithful priest who would do his will, with a sure house, walking before his anointed forever: 'and I will raise me up a faithful priest, that shall do according to that which is in mine heart and in my mind: and I will build him a sure house; and he shall walk before mine anointed for ever' — the faithful priest and his fulfilment in Christ — 'and I will raise me up a faithful priest, that shall do according to that which is in mine heart and in my mind: and I will build him a sure house; and he shall walk before mine anointed for ever': God promises, in place of the rejected house of Eli, a faithful priest with a sure and lasting house (the witnesses noting the significance — the gracious counterpart to the judgment, that God will not leave himself without a faithful priesthood, but will raise up a priest who does his will, build him a sure house, and set him to walk before God's Anointed forever, the witnesses seeing the promise fulfilled in stages: first in Zadok, who replaced the line of Eli, in Abiathar, and whose house held the priesthood, then more fully in the faithful priesthood God maintained, and at last and truly in Christ, the great High Priest who perfectly does the will of God, whose priesthood is everlasting and whose house is sure, walking before God's Anointed, or, being himself both Priest and the Anointed, forever, the faithful priest replacing the unfaithful, the sure house God builds, the everlasting priesthood fulfilled in Christ, the lesson — that God will always have a faithful ministry to himself, raising up those who do his will when others fail, 'I will give you pastors according to mine heart, which shall feed you with knowledge and understanding,' Jeremiah 3:15, the truth that the priesthood God establishes is sure and everlasting, resting on his own purpose, 'thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek,' Psalm 110:4, the figure — the faithful and everlasting High Priest, fulfilled in Christ who continues ever and has an unchangeable priesthood, 'this man, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood... able also to save them to the uttermost,' Hebrews 7:24-25, the truth that the faithful priest who does God's will and walks before his Anointed forever is Christ himself, the figure of the faithful priesthood God establishes in place of the unfaithful, fulfilled at last in Christ the great High Priest, the call to trust the faithful Priest God has raised up, the assurance that God's true priesthood stands forever in Christ); God raising up a faithful priest in place of Eli's house, one who does his will, fulfilled first in Zadok and the faithful priesthood (the faithful ministry, Jeremiah 3:15) — the promise (a faithful priest); 'and I will build him a sure house; and he shall walk before mine anointed for ever' (God building the faithful priest a sure house and setting him to walk before his Anointed forever, the everlasting priesthood fulfilled at last in Christ, the great High Priest of an unchangeable priesthood, the everlasting Priest, Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 7:24-25) — the figure (the faithful and everlasting High Priest, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the promise, the begging of the fallen house for bread to follow, vv.36) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Who is the faithful priest God promises to raise up?

sides.position: The faithful priesthood God establishes in place of the unfaithful, fulfilled by stages and at last in Christ the great High Priest - God raising a priest who does his will with a sure house walking before his Anointed forever, fulfilled first in Zadok who replaced Eli's line, then in the faithful priesthood, and truly in Christ who perfectly does God's will (Jeremiah 3:15), the everlasting priesthood (Psalm 110:4), Christ who has an unchangeable priesthood (Hebrews 7:24-25)

themes: And I will raise me up a faithful priest, that shall do according to that which is in mine heart and in my mind: and I will build him a sure house; and he shall walk before mine anointed for ever.

God's promise to raise up a faithful priest who would do his will, with a sure house, walking before his anointed forever (the crux of the faithful priesthood God establishes in place of the unfaithful, fulfilled in Christ)

the gracious counterpart to the judgment, that God will not leave himself without a faithful priesthood but will raise up a priest who does his will, build him a sure house, and set him before his Anointed forever

fulfilled by stages in Zadok and the faithful priesthood and at last in Christ, God always having a faithful ministry, the priesthood he establishes everlasting, fulfilled in Christ who has an unchangeable priesthood (Jeremiah 3:15; Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 7:24-25)

schools.summary: The fallen house of Eli reduced to begging for bread: 'and it shall come to pass, that every one that is left in thine house shall come and crouch to him for a piece of silver and a morsel of bread, and shall say, Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priests' offices, that I may eat a piece of bread' — Eli's house would be brought so low as to beg the faithful priest for bread (the and it shall come to pass, that every one that is left in thine house shall come and crouch to him for a piece of silver and a morsel of bread, the begging, the and shall say, Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priests' offices, that I may eat a piece of bread, the abasement, the figure, the proud and greedy house brought to beggary, the contempt requited, the truth that Eli's house, once fat with the offerings, would beg for bread, Proverbs 16:18, the figure of the despiser of God brought to shame and want, the abasement of the house, the begging for bread, vv.30); Eli's house begging for bread (the abasement, Proverbs 16:18) — the begging (crouch for bread); 'Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priests' offices, that I may eat a piece of bread' (the once-proud house begging a place for bread, the shame, the figure, the despiser of God brought to shame and want, the providence, the abasement, the call of Samuel and the fall of Eli's house to follow, ch.3-4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it shall come to pass, that every one that is left in thine house shall come and crouch to him for a piece of silver and a morsel of bread, and shall say, Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priests' offices, that I may eat a piece of bread.
The fallen house of Eli reduced to begging for bread
Eli's house, once fat with the offerings, brought so low as to beg the faithful priest for bread
the despiser of God brought to shame and want (Proverbs 16:18)
